item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dust mites are everywere and there are millions of them in even a cm2

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was quite into this…. until the HUGE estimated number taken at face-value in the report. Small detail for some, but kicked my suspension of disbelief quite hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I replaced "thoughts" with "mental activity" in the description. "Thoughts" does imply a little bit more purposeful intent and meaning than what you would actually find in the brain of an ant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not going to cast a vote for the time being but I nearly voted down. I appreciate being able to read thoughts but how can you read the thoughts of an animal without a cerebral cortex? Surely it would be more sensations of feeling and emotions.

I love the idea but I think its a little too unpredictable to get a decent set of results. As most animals tested cant generate thoughts of their own theoretically you'd get nothing but mental static. I'm not sure what some of the results you have mentioned refer to, it gives me no room for comparison.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excuse me, I'm an idiot, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Consuming one mayfly allowed the imbiber to hear the thoughts of other mayflies. I don't think it's inconsistent, although there may be a way to clarify it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Slight issue:

Contents: One mayfly (Ephemera danica), deceased
Result: Loud sobbing and hyperventilation, ending when the specimen expired.

Does that seem inconsistent to just me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this on the whole, but one thing I'm ambivalent about is the complexity of the criteria for what animals do and don't work. It makes me think, which is usually a plus in my book, but it makes me think about things which I'm not sure are really the 'point' of the SCP, if that makes sense? Kind of distracts from the point, in other words?

For example:

- Do controversial foods like oysters work for some people and not others?
- If an individual person is, for whatever reason, creeped out by ordinary animals such as dogs, would they be able to read dog thoughts by drinking dog hair?
- Can someone be creeped out by the human species? Could it be leveraged into human telepathy?
- Would it work on anomalous nonsentient animals or sentient beings, provided the drinker is sufficiently creeped out by them?

I am by far NOT an expert on writing good SCPs so I don't think that necessarily test logs need to be added to answer these questions, I'm just kinda expressing where my mind went, here

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes they can, and I added another test log to show that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New question, can someone use the effect multiple times to hear several species?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added an interview log to the Discovery section. Hopefully it improves it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Might work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if I added an interview with the initial victim or one of his family members that clarified a few things?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is what I was getting at. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was this close to downvoting, then I read the logs and upvoted.

To me the logs are what saves this. I liked the way that you portrayed alien thought patterns, especially the octopus. That last log can clear up how the anomalous effects were discovered, and I liked it when the guy could tell when there were spiders nearby.

The effect by itself isn't very interesting, but since you have the logs to go with it, I would leave it as is.

How it was recovered is a different story, many people will ask why someone put parts of an animal they were disgusted by in this weird glass. Even your possible spoilers beg the question why this guy wasn't just written off as crazy. In my opinion, this might do better if it had a believable recovery log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It came across as unclear to me while I was reading. I don't know, maybe I just get caught up in details.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Possible spoilers
- Possible spoilers

He didn't. He just didn't completely decontaminate it before he drank out of it, and so he started hearing the thoughts of all the tiny dust mites that were still stuck to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right, but who in their right mind would put animal parts in a glass? If that's what the original owner did for some reason after he discovered it in his attic, then I think you should include that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No no, you put a dead animal in the glass and eat it. The bugs trapped in the amber are purely decorative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
wat

So you have to ingest parts of an animal from the glass in order to experience these effects? How do those parts get there and who in their right mind would do that in the first place?
I don't know, maybe I'm missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My feelings kept ricocheting between "this is really interesting" and "omigosh EWW". Very nice. Perhaps add a single cute experiment result to increase the dissonance?
